title: Knowing your attachment pattern explains 80% of your relational confusion.
body: Secure, anxious, avoidant — three rough categories from decades of research. They describe how you reach for people when stressed, what you do when intimacy feels close, and why you keep ending up in the same arguments.
reflectionQuestion: Under stress, do you lean toward the person, pull away, or stay steady?

title: Your threatened self is not your usual self.
body: How you show up under fear / criticism / vulnerability is its own personality. Most relational damage happens at the hands of your threatened self while your usual self is offline. Recognising the shift is the work.
reflectionQuestion: What does your threatened self look like — and what does it tend to do that you regret?

title: What you can't stand in others is usually about you.
body: Not always, but often enough to be worth checking. The trait you find most intolerable in a colleague is sometimes a trait you fear in yourself, or one you were criticised for as a child. Curiosity beats certainty here.
reflectionQuestion: Who currently irritates you out of proportion — and what does the irritation say about you?

title: Your inner critic is a part, not the whole of you.
body: IFS terminology: the harsh inner voice is one part, often originally protective. Treating it as your true voice keeps you stuck. Treating it as one voice in a crowded room lets the other parts have a say.
reflectionQuestion: What does your inner critic sound like, and whose voice does it remind you of?

title: Your defensive moves are old protections, not character flaws.
body: Going silent, getting sharp, fleeing the thread, over-explaining — every defensive pattern started as a smart solution for a younger version of you. The problem is using a child's solution for an adult problem.
reflectionQuestion: What's your most common defensive move — and what was it originally protecting?

title: If you keep having the same argument, it's not about the dishwasher.
body: Recurring fights are usually two unsaid things wrapped in one observable thing. "You never listen" rarely means literally that. The real argument is underneath. Finding it requires both people stepping back.
reflectionQuestion: What recurring argument do you have with someone close — and what is it actually about?

title: You owe people honesty. You don't owe them comfort.
body: Adults can handle hearing a no, a disagreement, a hard truth. Protecting them from your real position is paternalistic, even when it feels like kindness. The boundary between honesty and cruelty is tone, not content.
reflectionQuestion: Whose comfort have you been protecting at the cost of your own honesty?

title: Loneliness is information about a need, not a verdict on your worth.
body: Loneliness research (Holt-Lunstad et al.) is unambiguous: chronic isolation has health effects comparable to heavy smoking. The feeling is signalling a real need. Treating it as a flaw makes you isolate further.
reflectionQuestion: What's your loneliness pointing at — a missing person, a missing kind of contact, both?

title: Forgiveness is not forgetting. It's setting down what you've been carrying.
body: You can forgive without reconciling. You can keep someone out of your life and still set down the weight of what they did. The decision is whether to keep paying interest on an injury that's already done.
reflectionQuestion: What injury are you still paying interest on — and what would setting it down look like?

title: Most lives have about five people in the front row.
body: Who is in your front row — the people whose opinion you actually weigh, whose calls you take, whose presence steadies you? Be honest, including the people who shouldn't be there anymore.
reflectionQuestion: Who's in your front row — and is everyone there earning their seat?

title: Your attachment pattern doesn't doom anything.
body: Anxious or avoidant patterns are tendencies, not life sentences. Earned security (research from Sue Johnson, Stan Tatkin) is real — adults can develop more secure patterns through relationships and reflection. The fact you're reading this is part of how.
reflectionQuestion: What's one small move toward security you could practice this week?

title: Other people are not projects.
body: Trying to fix the people in your life — even the difficult ones, even the addicts, even the family — usually fails and damages the relationship in the failing. The respect-based move is to be present, name what you see, and not be in charge of their work.
reflectionQuestion: Who in your life have you been trying to fix instead of just be present with?
